sentences.text: What's your name?
This is my handsome husband, Stuart Somerstein.

The experience of being married to someone who came through the prosecution of the Holocaust has helped me understand what human beings can do to one another and has helped me to understand my Jewishness a little bit more and to make me proud of being a Jew.
And I hope that for generations-- for time immemorial will understand the terrible genocide that was perpetrated on the Jewish race, and mankind, at that time.
And I hope that all generations will learn from it and not ever allow it to happen again.
My wife has [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] been scarred by this, and she has survived, obviously, very well.
And I hope that I've lent something to that because she has contributed greatly to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] happiness of society through her work and through her charitable efforts.
I thought it was important, after we got to know each other, that she admitted to me that she never spoke about it.
She never discussed her childhood with her children or with anyone or even her ex-husband, for that matter.

And I wanted to bring it out in her because I thought it would be important for her psychological well-being to be able to share this terrible tragedy with, not only myself, but with her children and the other people that she loves.
And I thought it would be important to go back to her roots, which where she never was until, oh, a good 30 years later-- maybe more.
And I was proud of doing that, but I was unhappy with the experience that she had when she returned home.
We drove from Budapest to Nyiergyhaza, and when we arrived there, she was very excited and happy to be back where she grew up.

And then, the realization of what happened there snapped in her brain, and it brought back such horrible memories that we had to leave because all she could remember were the Nazis and the terrible experience that happened from that point on.
But I think, it helped her understand what happened during that time.
And then, just two years ago, we went to a concentration camp in Mauthausen, where her father was killed, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING]
That's a terrible place.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
Sorry.

And the realization of seeing that was the worst experience of my life-- for her, sadness and just for the-- the-- the actuality of how anything like that could be perpetrated on humanity was just unbelievable to me.
I'm sorry.
But--
There were schoolchildren there, which was very nice to see.
You remember?
Yes.
Tell them about that.

What was more interesting, I thought-- well, as interesting as the schoolchildren-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every country, from Albania to Hungary and every Iron Curtain country and every Western European country, had erected monuments to the people that were lost.

And there were hundreds, literally hundreds, maybe thousands, of individual memorial plaques for people that were lost there-- from Spanish people to Ukrainian people to Austrian people, Jewish people, et cetera.
And that, really, is a living memorial to the unfortunate people that lost their lives there.
And it-- it just-- it was-- to see how this camp operated and to see the-- there was a cliff of, maybe, 300 feet down.
And on the cliff, they used to just line up the people and push them over.
It was-- it it was just unbelievable.
And the children that we saw there--
Were not Jewish children.
Were not Jewish children, and yet, they were--
Interested enough to come there, which was wonderful to see.
And to learn what it was like.
And I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what you are doing is-- is helping to show the generations of the future that this can never happen again.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
Amen.
Thank you.
